title: NDFC Flow Telemetry Drop and Export Health
app: Cisco DC Networking Application (Splunkbase 7777), NetFlow/IPFIX collector syslog
spl:
index=cisco_dc sourcetype="cisco:ndfc:flow_export" earliest=-4h
| stats sum(dropped_flows) as drops avg(export_rate_eps) as eps by switch_name, collector_ip
| where drops>0 OR eps < 100
| sort -drops
| table switch_name, collector_ip, drops, eps
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t avg(All_Traffic.bytes_in) as agg_value from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.action, All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - agg_value
description: Flow telemetry underpins capacity and security analytics for the NX-OS fabric. Collector drops or stalled exports create blind spots where congestion and microbursts go unseen until applications complain. Monitoring pipeline health preserves trust in east-west utilization dashboards.
value: Flow telemetry underpins capacity and security analytics for the NX-OS fabric. Collector drops or stalled exports create blind spots where congestion and microbursts go unseen until applications complain. Monitoring pipeline health preserves trust in east-west utilization dashboards.
implementation: (1) Ingest NDFC telemetry health API or collector events with per-switch counters. (2) Baseline `eps` per site. (3) Alert on drops or sustained low export rate; verify CPU and sampler intervals on switches.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
вАҐ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Cisco DC Networking Application (Splunkbase 7777), NetFlow/IPFIX collector syslog.
вАҐ Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=cisco_dc` `sourcetype="cisco:ndfc:flow_export"` with fields `switch_name`, `export_profile`, `dropped_flows`, `export_rate_eps`, `collector_ip`.
вАҐ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 вАФ Configure data collection
(1) Ingest NDFC telemetry health API or collector events with per-switch counters. (2) Baseline `eps` per site. (3) Alert on drops or sustained low export rate; verify CPU and sampler intervals on switches.

Step 2 вАФ Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=cisco_dc sourcetype="cisco:ndfc:flow_export" earliest=-4h
| stats sum(dropped_flows) as drops avg(export_rate_eps) as eps by switch_name, collector_ip
| where drops>0 OR eps < 100
| sort -drops
| table switch_name, collector_ip, drops, eps
```

Understanding this SPL

**NDFC Flow Telemetry Drop and Export Health** вАФ Flow telemetry underpins capacity and security analytics for the NX-OS fabric. Collector drops or stalled exports create blind spots where congestion and microbursts go unseen until applications complain. Monitoring pipeline health preserves trust in east-west utilization dashboards.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=cisco_dc` `sourcetype="cisco:ndfc:flow_export"` with fields `switch_name`, `export_profile`, `dropped_flows`, `export_rate_eps`, `collector_ip`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Cisco DC Networking Application (Splunkbase 7777), NetFlow/IPFIX collector syslog. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedвАФrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: cisco_dc; **sourcetype**: cisco:ndfc:flow_export. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

вАҐ Scopes the data: index=cisco_dc, sourcetype="cisco:ndfc:flow_export", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
вАҐ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by switch_name, collector_ip** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
вАҐ Filters the current rows with `where drops>0 OR eps < 100` вАФ typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
вАҐ Orders rows with `sort` вАФ combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
вАҐ Pipeline stage (see **NDFC Flow Telemetry Drop and Export Health**): table switch_name, collector_ip, drops, eps

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t avg(All_Traffic.bytes_in) as agg_value from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.action, All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - agg_value
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**NDFC Flow Telemetry Drop and Export Health** вАФ Flow telemetry underpins capacity and security analytics for the NX-OS fabric. Collector drops or stalled exports create blind spots where congestion and microbursts go unseen until applications complain. Monitoring pipeline health preserves trust in east-west utilization dashboards.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=cisco_dc` `sourcetype="cisco:ndfc:flow_export"` with fields `switch_name`, `export_profile`, `dropped_flows`, `export_rate_eps`, `collector_ip`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Cisco DC Networking Application (Splunkbase 7777), NetFlow/IPFIX collector syslog. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedвАФrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

вАҐ Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` вАФ enable acceleration for that model.
вАҐ Orders rows with `sort` вАФ combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 вАФ Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 вАФ Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Timechart (export rate), Table (switches with drops), Single value (total dropped flows).
visualization: Timechart (export rate), Table (switches with drops), Single value (total dropped flows).
